refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both directions of communication, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote scenarios. Log p95 latency metrics across multiple operational sessions. Validate that latency remains within bounds during peak system load and network stress conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Telemetry acquisition and transmission shall introduce latency ≤ 50 ms (sensor to network egress).
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transport shall deliver telemetry and control packets with latency ≤ 80 ms (agreed network conditions).
FR-NGHE-008.3: Operator control input processing and actuation command transmission shall introduce latency ≤ 40 ms (operator input to actuator command egress).
FR-NGHE-008.4: System shall monitor and report end-to-end latency in real-time to operator and safety systems.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency telemetry streaming with hardware-accelerated sensor polling (≤10 ms cycle), direct network buffering, and priority queuing; pair with predictive control algorithms to mask residual network jitter.

DP-B: Deploy edge computing at vehicle with local sensor fusion and control loop closure; transmit only high-level commands and aggregated telemetry over network; implement adaptive bitrate encoding to minimize payload size.

DP-C: Establish dedicated low-latency network path (QoS-prioritized, possibly separate physical link) for teleoperation traffic; implement redundant communication channels with automatic failover; use time-synchronized clocks for latency measurement and compensation.
DP-D: Combine DP-B (edge control) with DP-C (dedicated network path) to achieve latency budget through hybrid local autonomy and optimized remote supervision.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid architecture combining edge-based local control loop closure at the vehicle (DP-B) with a dedicated, QoS-prioritized network path and redundant communication channels (DP-C). This approach partitions the latency budget: vehicle-side sensor-to-control latency (≤50 ms via local processing), network transport (≤80 ms via dedicated path), and operator-to-command latency (≤40 ms via priority queuing). Adaptive bitrate encoding reduces payload size; time-synchronized clocks enable latency measurement and jitter compensation.

potential_sources: Coupling between edge autonomy (DP-B) and remote supervision: must define clear handoff semantics and mode transitions to avoid conflicting control commands.
Coupling between network QoS (DP-C) and application-level retry logic: must ensure retransmission policies do not exceed latency budget.
Coupling between time synchronization and clock drift: must validate that clock skew remains within acceptable bounds over extended operation.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design that decouples the latency problem into three independent sub-problems (vehicle processing, network transport, operator processing), each with its own latency budget. Couplings are manageable through clear interface contracts and mode management. No fundamental design contradiction identified.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are known and stable within agreed bounds. Risk: Uncharacterized network variability could violate latency budget; mitigation: continuous network monitoring and adaptive fallback to lower-bandwidth modes.

Assumption: Vehicle-side edge computing platform has sufficient processing capacity for local control loop closure. Risk: Overload or thermal throttling could increase latency; mitigation: capacity planning and thermal management design.

Assumption: Operator interface can reliably detect and respond to latency warnings. Risk: Operator confusion or delayed reaction to high-latency alerts; mitigation: human factors testing and clear HMI design.

Assumption: Time synchronization can be maintained to within ±10 ms across vehicle and operator stations. Risk: Clock drift or NTP failures could degrade latency measurement accuracy; mitigation: redundant time sources and drift monitoring.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly specify: (1) the latency budget allocation across the three sub-systems (vehicle, network, operator) with justification; (2) the fallback strategy if any sub-system exceeds its budget; (3) the adaptive bitrate encoding algorithm and its latency impact; (4) the time synchronization protocol and acceptable clock skew tolerance.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency control architecture, including: (1) telemetry acquisition and streaming logic at the vehicle, (2) network transport optimization (QoS, redundancy, adaptive encoding), (3) operator control input processing and command transmission, (4) latency monitoring and reporting. The module integrates with Vehicle (for sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (for latency-related safety alerts), and external Network Link (for communication).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Telemetry stream from vehicle sensors (camera, lidar, IMU, etc.) transmitted to operator station. Latency budget: ≤50 ms from sensor acquisition to network egress. Payload: adaptive bitrate encoded (target: <5 Mbps under nominal conditions).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands (steering, throttle, bucket control, etc.) received from operator station. Latency budget: ≤40 ms from operator input to actuator command egress. Protocol: priority-queued, with acknowledgment for safety-critical commands.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Real-time latency status and alerts transmitted to Safety Services. Triggers high-latency warnings (>120 ms p95) and critical alerts (>150 ms p95). Enables safety manager to intervene or trigger failsafe modes.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Dedicated, QoS-prioritized network path for teleoperation traffic. Supports redundant channels with automatic failover. Carries both Telemetry (out) and Tasking (in) streams. Latency budget: ≤80 ms round-trip under agreed network conditions.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse ownership and obscure the latency control architecture. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle owns the edge computing and sensor/actuator interfaces, but does not own the operator station, network transport optimization, or operator control processing. Latency control spans beyond vehicle scope. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation and latency control. It owns the end-to-end latency architecture, including vehicle-side telemetry streaming, network optimization, operator-side control processing, and latency monitoring. Excellent containment with minimal diffusion. Clear ownership boundary.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerting, but not the latency control mechanism itself. Teleoperation latency is a safety input to Safety Services, not a Safety Services responsibility. Minimal containment.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008 (Teleoperation latency). It has clear scope boundaries, owns all major latency control mechanisms (edge processing, network optimization, operator processing, monitoring), and has minimal diffusion risk. The module integrates with Vehicle (for sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (for latency alerts), and Network Link (for transport).
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may lack direct control over network infrastructure (QoS, redundancy). Mitigation: Define clear interface contract with Network Link; establish SLA for network latency performance.

Risk: Vehicle-side edge computing may be owned by Vehicle module, creating split ownership of latency control. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership: Teleoperation owns latency control logic; Vehicle owns hardware platform and sensor/actuator drivers.

Risk: Operator station hardware and HMI may be owned by external stakeholder (e.g., operator console manufacturer). Mitigation: Define latency requirements as part of operator station procurement specification.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign CR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module with explicit ownership of: (1) telemetry streaming logic and latency budgeting at vehicle, (2) network transport optimization and QoS management, (3) operator control processing and command transmission, (4) latency monitoring and reporting. Establish clear interface contracts with Vehicle (sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (latency alerts), and Network Link (transport SLA). Document assumptions about network conditions and operator station capabilities.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid edge control + dedicated network path) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for end-to-end latency ≤150 ms p95. The DP partitions the latency budget across three sub-systems (vehicle, network, operator), each with explicit latency targets. The DP is feasible, testable, and aligns with high safety criticality. No fundamental mismatch between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Edge computing logic (part of DP-B) may be split between Teleoperation module and Vehicle module. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership: Teleoperation owns latency control algorithms; Vehicle owns hardware platform.
Diffusion Risk 2: Network QoS and redundancy (part of DP-C) may be owned by external network infrastructure team. Mitigation: Define SLA and interface contract with Network Link module.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has excellent containment (0.95 score) for DP-D. The module owns the end-to-end latency control architecture and integrates cleanly with Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No major architectural misalignment.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-008 is verifiable via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can instrument telemetry streaming, network transport, and operator control processing to measure latency at each stage. Real-time latency monitoring (FR-NGHE-008.4) enables continuous verification during operation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency budget is clearly allocated: FR-NGHE-008.1 (≤50 ms vehicle), FR-NGHE-008.2 (≤80 ms network), FR-NGHE-008.3 (≤40 ms operator). Each sub-FR maps to a specific DP component and can be assigned to a sub-module or design team. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine DP-D to specify the latency budget allocation with engineering justification (e.g., why 50 ms for vehicle, 80 ms for network, 40 ms for operator).
Revision 2: Document the fallback strategy if any sub-system exceeds its latency budget (e.g., reduce bitrate, switch to lower-fidelity telemetry, trigger safety alert).
Revision 3: Establish explicit SLA with Network Link module for network latency performance (≤80 ms p95 under agreed conditions).
Revision 4: Define operator station requirements (latency budget, HMI responsiveness) as part of procurement specification or interface contract.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is a complex, safety-critical requirement that spans multiple sub-systems (vehicle, network, operator). The refined FR and selected DP are sound, but the requirement is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) to enable detailed design and verification. The Teleoperation module is the correct IM boundary, but sub-modules (e.g., Vehicle Telemetry Streaming, Network Transport Optimization, Operator Control Processing, Latency Monitoring) should be defined at the next decomposition level. Proceeding to the next level will clarify ownership, interfaces, and verification strategies for each latency control component.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Vehicle telemetry acquisition and transmission shall introduce latency ≤50 ms (sensor to network egress). [Owned by: Teleoperation + Vehicle integration]
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transport shall deliver telemetry and control packets with latency ≤80 ms (agreed network conditions). [Owned by: Teleoperation + Network Link integration]

FR-NGHE-008.3: Operator control input processing and actuation command transmission shall introduce latency ≤40 ms (operator input to actuator command egress). [Owned by: Teleoperation + operator station integration]
FR-NGHE-008.4: System shall monitor and report end-to-end latency in real-time to operator and safety systems. [Owned by: Teleoperation + Safety Services integration]

assumptions: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are known and stable within agreed bounds.
Vehicle-side edge computing platform has sufficient processing capacity for local control loop closure.
Operator interface can reliably detect and respond to latency warnings.
Time synchronization can be maintained to within ±10 ms across vehicle and operator stations.
Operator station hardware and HMI are procured or designed to meet latency requirements.
Network infrastructure (QoS, redundancy) can be configured to meet ≤80 ms latency SLA.
open_risks: Risk: Uncharacterized network variability could violate latency budget. Mitigation: Continuous network monitoring and adaptive fallback.
Risk: Vehicle-side processing overload or thermal throttling could increase latency. Mitigation: Capacity planning and thermal management.
Risk: Operator confusion or delayed reaction to latency alerts. Mitigation: Human factors testing and clear HMI design.
Risk: Clock drift or NTP failures could degrade latency measurement accuracy. Mitigation: Redundant time sources and drift monitoring.
Risk: Split ownership of latency control between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership and interface contracts.
evidence_needed: Network characterization study: bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles under agreed conditions.
Vehicle edge computing capacity analysis: processing power, memory, thermal limits.
Operator station latency budget specification: HMI responsiveness, input processing latency.
Time synchronization protocol specification: NTP configuration, clock skew tolerance.
Latency measurement methodology: instrumentation points, measurement accuracy, p95 calculation.
Operator acceptance testing results: subjective feedback on control responsiveness and safety.
Failover and fallback strategy documentation: mode transitions, bitrate adaptation, safety alerts.
